questions.questionText: The most common type of age-related cataract is:
questions.optionB: Nuclear sclerosis (aging nucleus becomes yellowish-brown → gradual loss of distance vision; causes 'index myopia' — second sight of the aged where near vision temporarily improves)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Nuclear sclerosis = most common senile cataract type. Why correct: Aging → protein aggregation in lens nucleus → yellowish-brown discoloration → central opacity. Causes index (lenticular) myopia → presbyopic patients can temporarily read without glasses ('second sight'). Why A wrong: Anterior subcapsular — associated with atopic dermatitis, eye rubbing. Why C wrong: Posterior subcapsular — steroid use, diabetes, radiation. Causes early glare/difficulty reading. Why D wrong: Lamellar — most common childhood cataract. Clinical relevance: Treatment: phacoemulsification + posterior chamber IOL (gold standard surgery). Pre-operative biometry: IOLMaster for IOL power calculation (SRK formula). Exam trap: 'Second sight' = nuclear sclerosis (temporary ability to read without presbyopic glasses). This always points to nuclear cataract. Memory aid: 'Nuclear = Nucleus turns brown, Near vision improves briefly (index myopia).' Reference: Khurana – Lens.
questions.conceptTag: Nuclear sclerosis cataract second sight index myopia
